perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party and its Representatives shall adopt and/or maintain security processes and procedures to safeguard the confidentiality of all Confidential Information received by the Disclosing Party using a reasonable degree of care, but not less than that degree of care used in safeguarding its own similar information or material;
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party and its Representatives may adopt security processes and procedures to safeguard the confidentiality of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the explicit requirement for maintaining security processes and procedures and weakens the standard of care. By removing the 'reasonable degree of care, but not less than that degree of care used in safeguarding its own similar information or material' clause, the contract provides very little legal recourse if the Receiving Party fails to adequately protect the confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.01 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota's Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By removing the explicit requirement for reasonable security measures, the modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets and potentially violates the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates security measures to protect confidential information, using a standard of care at least as high as the Receiving Party uses for its own information. The changed text removes this explicit requirement, only suggesting that the Receiving Party 'may adopt' security measures, weakening the protection of trade secrets. The Law Explanation suggests this may violate Minnesota's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By removing the specific requirement for security measures and the standard of care, the modified text weakens the duty to maintain secrecy, creating a contradiction with the intent, if not the explicit letter, of the Act as it pertains to misappropriation through improper means.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that use or disclosure of any Confidential Information in a manner inconsistent with this Agreement will give rise to irreparable injury for which: (a) money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by such party; (b) the other party may be entitled to specific performance and injunction and other equitable relief with respect to any such breach; (c) such remedies will not be the exclusive remedies for any such breach, but will be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity; and (d) in the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines in a final non-appealable order that one party, or any of its Representatives, has breached this Agreement, such party will be liable for reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred by the other party in connection with such litigation, including, but not limited to, any appeals.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that use or disclosure of any Confidential Information in a manner inconsistent with this Agreement will give rise to injury.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the acknowledgment that money damages may not be sufficient and removes the explicit entitlement to specific performance, injunction, and other equitable relief, as well as the clause requiring the breaching party to cover legal fees. These are standard remedies in trade secret cases, and their removal could limit the available legal recourse for the Disclosing Party, thus contradicting state law concerning remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02 allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the mention of specific performance and injunction, the changed text could be interpreted as limiting the availability of these remedies, contradicting the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes key remedies (specific performance, injunction, legal fees) typically available in trade secret misappropriation cases. Minnesota Statute § 325C.02 explicitly allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets, stating, "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." The omission of specific performance and injunction in the changed text directly contradicts this statutory provision by limiting the remedies available, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will take reasonable steps to ensure that its Representatives adhere to the terms of this Agreement. The Receiving Party will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may inform its Representatives of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the obligation on the Receiving Party. By changing "will take reasonable steps to ensure" to "may inform", it removes the responsibility of the Receiving Party to actively ensure their representatives comply with the NDA. This contradicts the principle of vicarious liability often applied in trade secret law, where companies are held responsible for the actions of their employees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.01 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Minnesota's interpretation of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, employers can be held liable for the misappropriation of trade secrets by their employees. By reducing the Receiving Party's obligation to ensure compliance by their representatives, the amended clause weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to seek recourse for misappropriation, which contradicts the implied protections offered by the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The original contract places a clear obligation on the Receiving Party to ensure its Representatives adhere to the agreement's terms, holding the Receiving Party responsible for any breaches. The changed text significantly weakens this obligation by stating the Receiving Party 'may inform' its Representatives, making compliance optional rather than mandatory. This creates a contradiction because under Minnesota Statute 325C.01, misappropriation of trade secrets can occur through 'improper means,' which includes 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By diminishing the Receiving Party's duty to ensure its representatives maintain secrecy, the amended clause weakens the Disclosing Party's protection against misappropriation, creating a conflict with the statute's intent to protect trade secrets. While the snippet doesn't explicitly detail employer liability, the explanation accurately reflects the legal understanding of liability for employee actions concerning trade secrets, making the reduced obligation a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will ensure that all documents, memoranda, notes and other writings or electronic records prepared by it that include or reflect any Confidential Information are returned or destroyed as directed by the Disclosing Party;
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party will make reasonable efforts to return documents containing Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This weakens the requirement to return or destroy confidential information. By changing 'will ensure that all documents...are returned or destroyed as directed by the Disclosing Party' to 'will make reasonable efforts to return documents,' it introduces ambiguity and reduces the Receiving Party's obligation to fully comply with the return or destruction of confidential information. This could violate provisions of the Minnesota Uniform Trade Secrets Act that necessitate active steps to protect trade secrets even after an agreement ends.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.01 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota's Uniform Trade Secrets Act implies a continuing duty to protect trade secrets. By only requiring "reasonable efforts" to return documents, the revised text fails to adequately address the need to actively prevent misappropriation of trade secrets after the agreement's termination, potentially conflicting with the protective spirit of the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to protect confidential information upon termination of the agreement. The original text mandates the return or destruction of all documents containing confidential information as directed by the disclosing party. The changed text only requires 'reasonable efforts' to return documents, which introduces ambiguity and falls short of the standard implied by Minnesota's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as explained in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is absent, Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as including the acquisition of a trade secret by 'improper means', or the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. The change weakens the protection and increases the risk of misappropriation after the agreement ends. Requiring only "reasonable efforts" to return documents lowers the standard of care compared to ensuring return or destruction. The original text had a strong obligation, while the changed text leaves room for interpretation and potentially insufficient action, contradicting the protective spirit of the Minnesota Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
